claims.text: A 2011 Dutch study connected mice to brain scanners and found that mice remained conscious for up to four seconds after decapitation.

evidence.quote:
in 2011 dutch scientists connected mice to a brain scanner and cut their heads off they discovered that mice remained conscious for up to four
seconds

claims.rationale: A 2011 Radboud University (Netherlands) EEG study of decapitated rats measured persistent cortical activity for roughly four seconds, often cited with the figure. The '4 seconds = conscious' inference oversimplifies: EEG activity does not strictly equal awareness, but the study itself is real.

claims.text: Other studies report small mammals remain aware for up to 29 seconds after decapitation, which if extrapolated to humans would allow for a grisly post-severance experience.

evidence.quote:
small mammals remained aware for up to 29 seconds now if this is also
true for humans it would provide enough time for a pretty gruesome experienc

claims.rationale: A 29-second figure is more extreme than the typical animal-decapitation literature, which reports seconds-scale (not half-minute-scale) cortical activity. The claim is likely an upper-bound outlier or misattribution. Extrapolating from rat brain mass to human brain mass is not straightforward either.

claims.text: During the French Revolution a man retrieved a severed head just after the guillotine fell and the severed head communicated via blinking and facial expressions with its still-living friend.

evidence.quote: some have even made sounds despite being severed from the rest of their body there's even a famous story about a man who attended the execution of his friend d

claims.rationale: This sounds like a conflation/embellishment of the Charlotte Corday and Henri Languille cases. There is no verified French Revolution account of a severed head blinking on command to answer a series of questions; the story circulates in popular anthologies without a primary source.

claims.text: The phenomenon of apparent awareness in a severed head is known as 'lucid decapitation'.

evidence.quote:
the victim allegedly communicated with his friend this type of post head chopped off consciousness is called lucid decapitation so
scientists and doctors starte

claims.rationale: 'Lucid decapitation' is an uncommon term of art not widely used in the medical literature. Post-decapitation residual brain activity is real but not typically labeled this way in peer-reviewed sources — the term's usage here is idiosyncratic.

claims.text: When the executioner's assistant slapped Charlotte Corday's severed head in 1793 after her guillotining, eyewitnesses reported her face took on an angry, aware expression.

evidence.quote:
charlotte corday was guillotined in 1793
for murder the executioner's assistant lifted her head by the hair and slapped it on both cheeks for some reason and

claims.rationale: Corday's execution is documented (July 17, 1793, for the murder of Marat). The executioner's-assistant-slap detail and blush anecdote are period accounts of variable reliability; French doctors at the time already contested the 'angry expression' as nervous spasm rather than voluntary motion.

claims.text: Contemporaries attributed Corday's apparent facial reaction to involuntary nervous spasms rather than voluntary action by a conscious head.

evidence.quote:
situation the it means humiliating i know what it means anyway french doctors argue that witnesses were
confusing nervous spasms with voluntary mo

claims.rationale: Postural and facial nerve spasms in the minutes following severance are well-established neurophysiology. Conflating them with intentional expression remains a common perceptual error and is the standard skeptical reading.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] Offers reflexive neurological spasms as a parsimonious explanation that displaces 'conscious severed head'.

claims.text: In 1905 French doctor Beaurieux investigated the severed head of guillotined murderer Henri Languille and reported that the eyes opened and fixed focus on his when the name was called.

evidence.quote:
05 when
a french doctor named barrow was allowed to make an investigation of a severed head his um
subject was a criminal named henri lungieel and immediately a

claims.rationale: The 1905 Beaurieux–Languille case is a documented and frequently cited historical anecdote in capital-punishment literature. The account is a single published observer report, not a replicated experiment; modern neurology would attribute the eye movements to reflexive not voluntary behavior.

claims.text: A 1950s study by French doctors Piedelièvre and Fournier concluded that death by guillotine is not instantaneous and that 'every vital element survives decapitation.'

evidence.quote:
s its supporters claimed in the 1950s a study by doctors pio de
livre and fournier concluded that death by guillotine is not instantaneous the report said every

claims.rationale: The Piedelièvre & Fournier 1956 report to the French Academy of Medicine is historically attested. The 'every vital element' quote is widely reproduced. The conclusion that guillotining is non-instantaneous is consistent with modern neurophysiology.

claims.text: Tunisian-born killer Hamida Djandoubi was the last person guillotined in France, in 1977, and the last executed in Western Europe.

evidence.quote:
to be guillotined in france was tunisian-born killer hamida danjubi in 1977. don jubi
became the last person in western europe to be executed by the state befor

claims.rationale: Djandoubi's September 10, 1977 execution at Baumettes Prison in Marseille is the well-documented final guillotining in France and in Western Europe (preceding abolition in 1981).

claims.text: France officially abolished capital punishment in 1981.

evidence.quote:
fficially abolished capital punishment in 1981. the french
authorities had already banned public executions after the heading of eugene wiedman in 193

claims.rationale: The loi Badinter abolishing the death penalty in France was enacted October 9, 1981. This is a well-known, uncontested historical fact.

claims.text: French authorities banned public executions after the 1939 beheading of Eugen Weidmann at Versailles, which was secretly filmed and widely seen.

evidence.quote:
dy banned public executions after the heading of eugene wiedman in 1939 this event was secretly filmed by a member of the crowd in
versailles and once the horri

claims.rationale: Eugen Weidmann's June 17, 1939 execution at Saint-Pierre Prison in Versailles was the last public guillotining in France and was covertly filmed. France moved all subsequent executions behind prison walls.

claims.text: The severed head of Diogo Alves, Portugal's first serial killer executed by hanging in 1841, is still preserved in a glass jar at the University of Lisbon's Faculty of Medicine 150 years later.

evidence.quote:
bon's facility of medicine diogo alves was portugal's first serial killer and one of the last people to be
executed in the country in 1841. his severed head was

claims.rationale: Alves's preserved head in a formalin jar at the University of Lisbon's Faculty of Medicine is a real, photographed anatomical specimen often discussed in Portuguese historical tourism. Alves was executed in 1841; the preservation is uncontested.

claims.text: Some doctors estimate a human head could survive up to 15 minutes after decapitation, but realistically consciousness fades within a few seconds as the brain runs out of oxygen (anoxia).

evidence.quote:
aid a human head could
survive up to 15 minutes but it's probably just a few seconds when the brain runs out of oxygen this is called anoxia and all motor funct

claims.rationale: The 'few seconds' timescale is the modern consensus — cerebral oxygen stores deplete in roughly 10 seconds after blood flow stops, producing rapid loss of consciousness. The 15-minute upper bound is a fringe outlier and almost certainly incorrect.

claims.text: Miracle Mike was a rooster owned by Colorado farmer Lloyd Olsen who continued to live, walk, and peck for food after Olsen chopped his head off.

evidence.quote:
acle mike was a chicken who belonged to a farmer in colorado named lloyd olson and lloyd was planning on having a little chicken dinner but after chopping
off m

claims.rationale: Mike the Headless Chicken (Fruita, Colorado, 1945) is a well-documented case — the cut apparently left the brain stem intact, allowing involuntary behavior to persist. Mike toured nationally and was featured in Life magazine.

claims.text: Miracle Mike lived for 18 more months without his head, was featured on tour and on the cover of Time Life magazine, and died after a corn kernel got stuck in his neck.

evidence.quote:
n crazier is miracle mike lived for 18 more months without his head he went on tour made public appearances he was
even on the cover of time life magazine that'

claims.rationale: The ~18-month survival, national tour, and Life magazine coverage are documented. The exact cause of death (choking on a corn kernel or a similar aspiration event) is the commonly cited cause in archival accounts.
